hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Ontario Cottage (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century Ontario Cottage House-form building that reflects early workers’ housing within the District. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s, which included a large concentration of workers’ housing. Constructed as a row, the properties retain their original composition of a central gable and entrance flanked by windows to either side. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: Local | Residential | Transition to Bellevue Park

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Poor condition, cluttered yard with stored items, chain-link fence, general neglect visible
photo_observations.window_details: Small windows flanking central door, some appear to be enclosed/boarded, tan/beige infill panel beside door
photo_observations.door_details: White front door, central position under gable peak, with sidelights or panels
photo_observations.porch_details: No porch, direct entry at grade
photo_observations.chimney_details: Single brick chimney visible at roof ridge, center/rear position
photo_observations.decorative_details: Central gable form only, minimal surviving decoration
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Tan/beige infill panel beside door (possible window bricked up), chain-link fence, general neglect
photo_observations.notes: Ontario Cottage in poorest condition of the row. Chimney visible on ridge. Yard cluttered with stored items. Central gable form still readable but details deteriorated.

facade_description: 136 Denison Ave presents a row-house facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 1 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, row, ontario cottage typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using casement, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century Ontario Cottage House-form building that reflects early workers’ housing within the District. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s, which included a large concentration of workers’ housing. Constructed as a row, the properties retain their original composition of a central gable and entrance flanked by windows to either side. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
